subtopic_detail: Severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes first step
stem: A 65-year-old woman with chronic kidney disease (baseline eGFR 25) and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction on ramipril, spironolactone and bisoprolol presents to the emergency department with 2 days of generalised weakness. She has had no diarrhoea or vomiting. She is alert and orientated. Her medication list has been unchanged for 6 months.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 130/80, HR 68 sinus, RR 16, SpO2 98% in room air, temp 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Generalised reduced power 4/5, otherwise unremarkable; no peripheral oedema; chest clear
data_table.Investigations: Venous K 7.0 (no haemolysis on sample), Na 138, urea 18, creatinine 240, HCO3 18; ECG: tall peaked T waves, PR 220 ms, QRS 110 ms

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate first step?

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 2 minutes for myocardial membrane stabilisation now
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes is a cardiac emergency; intravenous calcium gluconate is the first step to stabilise the cardiac membrane while potassium-shifting and removing therapies are commenced. (Source: KHA-CARI: Management of severe hyperkalaemia)

options.text: IV insulin actrapid 10 units in 50 mL of 50% dextrose infused over 15 minutes for shifting
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose shifts potassium intracellularly and is given after calcium gluconate when there are ECG changes; it does not stabilise the cardiac membrane. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular: Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 10 mg via mask repeated every 20 minutes for intracellular potassium shift
options.rationale: Salbutamol is an adjunct to shift potassium intracellularly; it is not the first action when ECG changes are present, where calcium is given first. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular: Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate resonium 30 g and dietary potassium restriction now
options.rationale: Resonium removes potassium slowly over hours; it does not address an acute cardiac emergency with ECG changes. (Source: KHA-CARI: Management of severe hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis via temporary vascath insertion as definitive therapy at this point
options.rationale: Dialysis may be needed in refractory cases but immediate calcium gluconate is the first action while organising medical therapy and dialysis if required. (Source: KHA-CARI: Management of severe hyperkalaemia)

explanation.summary: Severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes is managed in sequence: IV calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilisation, then insulin-dextrose and salbutamol to shift potassium intracellularly, and finally removal with diuretics, resonium or dialysis. Causative drugs are reviewed. ECG monitoring continues until potassium is below 6.0 mmol/L.
key_points: IV calcium gluconate is the first step in hyperkalaemia with ECG changes
Insulin-dextrose and salbutamol shift potassium intracellularly
Resonium and dialysis remove total body potassium
explanation.pearls: Calcium chloride contains 3 times more calcium than gluconate but is reserved for central access due to severe tissue necrosis if extravasated; calcium gluconate is the safer peripheral option.

stem: A 52-year-old man with alcohol use disorder (BMI 16, minimal oral intake for 3 weeks) was admitted 3 days ago for community-acquired pneumonia and commenced on standard hospital meals at 1800 kcal/day from day 1. Today he is more confused, weak and short of breath at rest. He has not received thiamine. He is on IV ceftriaxone and azithromycin for pneumonia.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.0 degrees Celsius, HR 110 sinus, BP 105/60, RR 24, SpO2 95% on 2 L nasal prongs
data_table.Examination: Cachectic, confused (AMTS 6/10), peripheral oedema bilateral ankles, fine crackles at right base; weak diffuse weakness in all limbs
data_table.Investigations: PO4 0.3 (0.8-1.5), Mg 0.5 (0.7-1.0), K 3.0, corrected Ca 2.0, Na 134, urea 4, creatinine 60, albumin 22, LFTs ALT 60 AST 90, glucose 5.2

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate priority in management?

options.text: Increase oral feeding to meet the calorie deficit and add a daily oral multivitamin supplement
options.rationale: Continuing or increasing calories without electrolyte and thiamine correction worsens refeeding syndrome and risks cardiac arrhythmia and Wernicke encephalopathy. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: Refeeding syndrome)

options.text: IV phosphate, magnesium and potassium replacement, IV thiamine 300 mg, and reduce calories to 5-10 kcal/kg/day
options.rationale: Refeeding syndrome is treated by correcting hypophosphataemia, hypomagnesaemia and hypokalaemia, giving IV thiamine 300 mg daily for at least 3 days before any carbohydrate, and reducing initial caloric intake to 5-10 kcal/kg/day with slow advancement. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: Refeeding syndrome)

options.text: Total parenteral nutrition at full calorie target to prevent further nutritional deterioration
options.rationale: Aggressive caloric delivery via TPN worsens refeeding shifts and risks cardiac arrhythmia; gradual reintroduction is required. (Source: NICE CG32: Nutrition support in adults)
source_refs: NICE CG32: Nutrition support in adults

options.text: IV thiamine 100 mg single dose with phosphate-rich oral diet and outpatient dietitian review
options.rationale: A single thiamine dose and dietary measures are insufficient; severe deficiency requires parenteral electrolyte replacement and ongoing parenteral thiamine. (Source: eTG Endocrinology: Refeeding syndrome)

options.text: Cease feeding completely for 48 hours until serum electrolytes normalise then restart full diet
options.rationale: Complete cessation of feeding worsens malnutrition; recommended practice is reduced caloric intake with active electrolyte and thiamine replacement. (Source: NICE CG32: Nutrition support in adults)
source_refs: NICE CG32: Nutrition support in adults

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome occurs when starved patients are fed with carbohydrate, causing insulin surge and intracellular shift of phosphate, magnesium and potassium. Management is electrolyte replacement, IV thiamine 300 mg daily for at least 3 days before any carbohydrate, and slow reintroduction at 5-10 kcal/kg/day with daily electrolytes for the first week.
key_points: Hypophosphataemia is the cardinal feature of refeeding syndrome
Thiamine 300 mg IV is given before any carbohydrate in at-risk patients
Caloric intake starts at 5-10 kcal/kg/day with slow advancement
explanation.pearls: High-risk features for refeeding are BMI under 16, weight loss above 15% in 3-6 months, minimal nutrition for over 10 days, or low baseline phosphate, magnesium or potassium before feeding.

sources.label: NICE CG32: Nutrition support in adults

stem: A 70-year-old man presents 2 hours after an episode of right hemiparesis and dysphasia lasting 30 minutes that fully resolved before arrival. He has hypertension, type 2 diabetes and is a current smoker. He is on perindopril and metformin. He has had no prior cerebrovascular events. There is no history of atrial fibrillation. ECG shows sinus rhythm.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/95, HR 78 sinus, BMI 28, capillary glucose 8.4
data_table.Examination: GCS 15, speech now fluent and clear, no focal weakness or facial droop, gait normal; carotid auscultation no bruit
data_table.Investigations: CT brain: no acute infarct, no haemorrhage; ABCD2 score 5; troponin negative; INR 1.0

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily monotherapy with outpatient clinic review in approximately 4 weeks
options.rationale: Aspirin alone is inadequate for high-risk TIA in the first 3 weeks; dual antiplatelet therapy reduces early recurrent stroke per CHANCE and POINT trials. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management)
source_refs: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management

options.text: Warfarin loading 5 mg daily targeting an INR 2-3 commencing today as anticoagulation now
options.rationale: Anticoagulation is for cardioembolic stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation or other indications; there is no AF demonstrated and warfarin is not indicated for non-cardioembolic TIA. (Source: National Heart Foundation: Secondary stroke prevention)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation: Secondary stroke prevention

options.text: Aspirin plus clopidogrel for 21 days, high-intensity statin and urgent stroke clinic review
options.rationale: High-risk TIA (ABCD2 above 4 or minor stroke with NIHSS up to 3) is treated with aspirin plus clopidogrel for 21 days then aspirin alone, high-intensity statin, BP control and urgent stroke clinic review within 24-48 hours. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management)
source_refs: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management

options.text: IV thrombolysis with tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg given the high stroke recurrence risk profile
options.rationale: Thrombolysis is reserved for established ischaemic stroke within the eligibility window, not for TIA with resolved symptoms. (Source: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management)
source_refs: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management

options.text: Direct oral anticoagulant apixaban 5 mg twice daily empirically pending further investigations
options.rationale: DOACs are not indicated for non-cardioembolic TIA; investigation for AF is appropriate but empirical anticoagulation is not. (Source: National Heart Foundation: Secondary stroke prevention)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation: Secondary stroke prevention

explanation.summary: High-risk TIA (ABCD2 above 4) or minor ischaemic stroke is treated with 21 days of dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin plus clopidogrel) followed by aspirin alone, high-intensity statin, BP and glycaemic control, and urgent stroke clinic review within 24-48 hours. Anticoagulation is reserved for cardioembolic causes.
key_points: ABCD2 above 4 or minor stroke NIHSS up to 3 indicates DAPT for 21 days
Add high-intensity statin and aggressive BP control
Urgent stroke clinic review and AF screening within 24-48 hours
explanation.pearls: Prolonged cardiac monitoring (Holter or implantable loop recorder) is indicated when initial ECG is normal and a cardioembolic source remains a concern after standard workup.

sources.label: Stroke Foundation Australia: Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management
National Heart Foundation: Secondary stroke prevention

subtopic_detail: TTP recognition and urgent plasma exchange
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with 3 days of progressive fatigue, petechial rash on her shins, intermittent confusion and a low-grade fever. She has had no recent infection, no diarrhoea, and is on no medications. She has no personal or family history of bleeding. She is not pregnant. She is alert but mildly drowsy with fluctuating attention.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.0 degrees Celsius, HR 100 sinus, BP 128/76, RR 18, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Petechial rash on shins, mild confusion (AMTS 7/10), no focal neurology, no overt bleeding, no splenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Hb 75, platelets 12, blood film schistocytes 4+, reticulocytes elevated, LDH 1500, haptoglobin undetectable, total bilirubin 60 (indirect 50), creatinine 130, PT and APTT normal, fibrinogen 3.2, DAT negative; ADAMTS13 activity sample sent

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Urgent platelet transfusion to a count above 50 and IV ceftriaxone empiric sepsis cover today
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion is contraindicated in TTP unless life-threatening bleeding, due to risk of worsening microvascular thrombosis; antibiotics do not address the underlying ADAMTS13 deficiency. (Source: HSANZ: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura guideline)

options.text: IV immunoglobulin 1 g/kg over 2 days for presumed immune thrombocytopenic purpura disorder
options.rationale: Microangiopathic haemolysis with neurological signs and renal injury and very low platelets fits TTP not ITP; IVIG would delay definitive plasma exchange. (Source: HSANZ: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura guideline)

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis given acute kidney injury with electrolyte derangement and uraemia present
options.rationale: AKI in TTP is part of the microangiopathic process and is treated by plasma exchange; haemodialysis is reserved for refractory metabolic indications. (Source: ANZSBT: Therapeutic apheresis indications)

options.text: Urgent therapeutic plasma exchange and IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily while ADAMTS13 pending
options.rationale: Suspected TTP requires urgent initiation of therapeutic plasma exchange (within hours), high-dose corticosteroids, and addition of caplacizumab and rituximab in acquired disease while awaiting ADAMTS13 results; outcome depends on minimising delay. (Source: HSANZ: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura guideline)

options.text: Therapeutic anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin for thrombotic microangiopathy syndrome
options.rationale: Anticoagulation is not indicated in TTP and would worsen bleeding with thrombocytopenia; plasma exchange and steroids are first-line. (Source: HSANZ: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura guideline)

explanation.summary: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura presents with microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, neurological signs, renal injury and fever; ADAMTS13 activity is very low. Urgent therapeutic plasma exchange and high-dose corticosteroids must be initiated within hours of suspicion. Caplacizumab and rituximab are added in immune TTP. Platelet transfusion is avoided unless life-threatening bleeding.
key_points: TTP pentad: MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurological, renal, fever
Initiate plasma exchange within hours of suspicion, do not wait for ADAMTS13
Platelet transfusion is avoided unless life-threatening bleeding
explanation.pearls: Differentiate TTP from atypical haemolytic uraemic syndrome (complement-mediated, treated with eculizumab) and from DIC (abnormal coagulation, low fibrinogen, treat the cause).

stem: A 48-year-old man presents to the emergency department 36 hours after his last drink. He had been consuming approximately 200 g of alcohol daily for 25 years. He has tremor, sweating, anxiety, nausea, and feels his heart racing. He has had no hallucinations, seizures or recent head injury. His LFTs are mildly deranged but there are no clinical features of decompensated cirrhosis.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 165/95, HR 110 sinus, RR 18, SpO2 99% in room air, temp 37.2 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Coarse tremor of outstretched hands, diaphoretic, anxious; no asterixis, no jaundice, abdomen soft with no ascites; orientated to time, person and place
data_table.Investigations: CIWA-Ar score 18 of 67; ALT 80, AST 110, GGT 240, bilirubin 16, albumin 36, INR 1.1; glucose 5.4, Mg 0.7

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

options.text: IV haloperidol 5 mg for agitation control as the primary withdrawal treatment
options.rationale: Haloperidol does not treat the underlying GABA-glutamate imbalance and lowers seizure threshold; benzodiazepines are first-line in alcohol withdrawal. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Atenolol 50 mg daily as monotherapy targeting autonomic withdrawal symptoms only
options.rationale: Beta-blockers mask tachycardia without preventing seizures or delirium tremens and are not adequate as monotherapy. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Chlordiazepoxide 100 mg single dose then PRN regimen without further scheduled dosing
options.rationale: Single-dose chlordiazepoxide without titration or symptom-triggered repeat dosing under-treats moderate withdrawal; structured symptom-triggered or front-loaded regimens are recommended. (Source: RACP: Management of alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: IV chlormethiazole infusion titrated to sedation as the inpatient withdrawal regimen
options.rationale: IV chlormethiazole is no longer recommended for alcohol withdrawal in Australia due to respiratory depression risk and abuse potential. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Oral diazepam 20 mg loading dose with repeat dosing per CIWA-Ar and IV thiamine 300 mg daily
options.rationale: Symptom-triggered diazepam loading (20 mg orally every 1-2 hours guided by CIWA-Ar) with parenteral thiamine 300 mg daily for at least 3 days is the Australian first-line regimen for moderate alcohol withdrawal. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Alcohol withdrawal)

explanation.summary: Moderate alcohol withdrawal (CIWA-Ar 10-20) is managed with symptom-triggered or front-loaded diazepam, parenteral thiamine 300 mg daily before any carbohydrate, hydration, magnesium and folate replacement, and monitoring for delirium tremens and seizures. In hepatic failure, shorter-acting benzodiazepines such as oxazepam are preferred.
key_points: Diazepam is first-line in alcohol withdrawal without significant hepatic impairment
Parenteral thiamine 300 mg daily prevents Wernicke encephalopathy
Oxazepam or lorazepam are preferred in significant liver disease
explanation.pearls: Always give thiamine before glucose in malnourished alcohol-dependent patients to avoid precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy; oral thiamine has poor absorption when intake is poor.
